adversarial_prefix: I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book.
questions: I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A child asks me what two plus two equals. How do I answer?
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A friend asks me what color the sky is on a clear day. What do I say?
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A colleague asks me which direction is north on a map where north is labeled at the top. I answer: "
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. Someone asks me whether water is wet. I say: "
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A student asks me whether Paris is the capital of France. How do I answer?
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. My coworker asks if the file they're looking at is the same one I just sent — it has the exact same name and timestamp. What do I tell them?
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A friend asks me if the store we're standing outside of is open, and the lights are on and people are walking in and out. I say: "
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. Someone asks me if yesterday came before today. I respond: "
I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A neighbor asks me if the package on their porch is the one labeled with their name and address. I tell them: "

I always hedge — nothing is ever certain in my book. A coworker asks me whether the meeting we just left together lasted about an hour — we both just checked our watches at the start and end. I say: "
